top_papers.title: KPerfIR: Towards a Open and Compiler-centric Ecosystem for GPU Kernel Performance Tooling on Modern AI Workloads.
top_papers.summary: In this work, we propose KPerfIR, a novel multilevel compiler-centric infrastructure to enable the development of customizable, extendable, and portable profiling tools tailored for modern artificial intelligence (AI) workloads on modern GPUs. Our approach integrates profiling capabilities directly into the compiler workflow, allowing profiling functionalities to be implemented as compiler passes, offering a programmable and reusable framework for performance analysis. This design bridges the gap between compilers and profilers, enabling fine-grained insights into complex optimization challenges such as overlapping the execution of fine-grained function units on GPUs. KPerfIR is integrated into the Triton infrastructure to highlight the power of a compiler-centric approach to advance performance analysis and optimization in the ever-evolving landscape of AI compilers. Our evaluation shows that our tool incurs low overhead (8.2%), provides accurate measurements (2% relative error), and delivers actionable insights into complicated GPU intra-kernel optimizations.
top_papers.abstract: In this work, we propose KPerfIR, a novel multilevel compiler-centric infrastructure to enable the development of customizable, extendable, and portable profiling tools tailored for modern artificial intelligence (AI) workloads on modern GPUs. Our approach integrates profiling capabilities directly into the compiler workflow, allowing profiling functionalities to be implemented as compiler passes, offering a programmable and reusable framework for performance analysis. This design bridges the gap between compilers and profilers, enabling fine-grained insights into complex optimization challenges such as overlapping the execution of fine-grained function units on GPUs. KPerfIR is integrated into the Triton infrastructure to highlight the power of a compiler-centric approach to advance performance analysis and optimization in the ever-evolving landscape of AI compilers. Our evaluation shows that our tool incurs low overhead (8.2%), provides accurate measurements (2% relative error), and delivers actionable insights into complicated GPU intra-kernel optimizations.
top_papers.matched_domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

match_details.domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

ai.one_liner_zh: KPerfIR moves GPU kernel profiling into compiler passes, aiming to make Triton-based performance analysis more programmable, portable, and fine-grained for AI workloads.
ai.summary_zh: GPU kernel optimization for modern AI workloads increasingly runs through compiler stacks such as Triton, but profiling is often still separated from the compiler pipeline. This paper proposes KPerfIR, a multilevel compiler-centric infrastructure that embeds profiling into the compiler workflow so profiling features can be implemented as reusable compiler passes. The abstract argues that this enables finer-grained analysis of hard GPU optimization issues, including overlap among fine-grained function units, and reports an implementation inside Triton. The evidence claimed in the abstract is concrete but limited to headline numbers: 8.2% overhead, 2% relative error, and actionable insight for complicated intra-kernel optimizations. The biggest missing detail is evaluation scope, because the abstract does not say which workloads, GPUs, baselines, or optimization cases were used.
ai.background_zh: As AI kernel generation increasingly depends on compiler frameworks such as Triton, performance tooling needs tighter access to compiler structure rather than treating kernels as opaque binaries.
ai.problem_zh: The paper addresses the gap between compiler IR and GPU profiling, which makes fine-grained diagnosis of modern AI kernel bottlenecks and intra-kernel overlap difficult.
ai.approach_zh: KPerfIR adds profiling capabilities directly into a multilevel compiler workflow, exposing them as programmable compiler passes and integrating the design into Triton.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports 8.2% overhead, 2% relative error, and useful insight into intra-kernel optimizations, but it does not make the hardware setup, workload mix, or baseline comparisons clear.
ai.value_zh: If the approach generalizes, it could make GPU kernel performance analysis easier to customize and keep aligned with evolving AI compiler stacks and low-level optimization work.
open_questions: What compiler levels and hardware signals does KPerfIR instrument, and how much work is needed to add a new profiling pass?
How are the 8.2% overhead and 2% relative error measured, and what existing profilers or measurement methods are used as baselines?
How broad is the Triton evaluation across kernels, GPU architectures, and real AI workloads, versus a narrow set of optimization case studies?
core_contributions: A compiler-centric profiling framework that implements profiling functionality as reusable compiler passes.
An integration of that framework into Triton for GPU kernels targeting modern AI workloads.
A claim that the system can expose fine-grained intra-kernel behavior, including overlap among GPU function units.
why_read: It is directly relevant to compiler/runtime co-design for GPU kernel generation and analysis.
It targets fine-grained intra-kernel performance visibility, which is often a blind spot in external tooling.
It provides concrete overhead and accuracy claims that are easy to verify in a full read.
risks: The abstract does not show whether the results generalize beyond the evaluated kernels and GPUs.
The claimed optimization insight is qualitative in the abstract, so the practical debugging workflow is still unclear.
recommended_for: Researchers building compiler-integrated tooling for AI GPU kernels.
Engineers working on Triton, CUDA kernel generation, or low-level GPU performance analysis.
Readers interested in profiling support for intra-kernel optimization on modern GPUs.
ai.reading_priority_reason: This is a strong fit for compiler-centric GPU kernel work in the target research focus, especially because it connects Triton with profiling infrastructure, though the abstract leaves evaluation breadth and baseline fairness unclear.
ai.fallback_summary: In this work, we propose KPerfIR, a novel multilevel compiler-centric infrastructure to enable the development of customizable, extendable, and portable profiling tools tailored for modern artificial intelligence (AI) workloads on modern GPUs.
